title: Design a User Registration API
problemStatement: Design a RESTful API for user registration and profile management. Users sign up with email and password, can view and update their profile, and change their password.

resourceSchema.constraints: Must be unique, valid email format
Min 8 characters, never returned in responses

functionalRequirements: User registration with email and password
constraints: Email must be unique and valid format
Password must be at least 8 characters
Profile updates should not allow changing email

rubric.reasoning: Auth APIs need registration, login, profile read/update, and password change. Missing login means no way to get tokens. Missing password change forces users to contact support.
POST for login creates a token (new resource). GET for profile is a safe, idempotent read. PUT for profile replaces the entire profile. Some teams use PATCH for partial profile updates.
409 Conflict for duplicate email is more precise than 400 — it tells the client exactly what's wrong. 401 for all auth failures (missing, invalid, expired tokens). Never 403 for login failures.

Duplicate email must be caught at registration. Password validation should enforce rules without leaking what rules exist (don't say 'must contain uppercase' in login errors). Wrong current password on change prevents account takeover.
Profile and password endpoints require auth — without it, anyone can read/modify any profile. Register and login are public by definition (user doesn't have a token yet).
Returning passwords (even hashed) in any response is a critical security vulnerability. Login should return a token with expiration info so clients know when to refresh.

defendQuestions.question: How would you store passwords on the server? Why not store them in plain text?
defendQuestions.modelReasoning: Never store plain text passwords. Use bcrypt or Argon2 to hash passwords with a unique salt per user. If your database is breached, hashed passwords can't be reversed to plain text (unlike encryption, which can be decrypted with the key). Bcrypt is deliberately slow to make brute-force attacks impractical. Always hash server-side, never rely on client-side hashing alone.

defendQuestions.question: What happens when a user's token expires? Should they have to log in again with their password?
defendQuestions.modelReasoning: Short-lived access tokens (15-60 min) paired with long-lived refresh tokens (days/weeks). When the access token expires, the client uses the refresh token to get a new access token without re-entering credentials. If the refresh token is also expired, THEN the user must log in again. This balances security (short access window) with UX (no constant re-login). Store refresh tokens in httpOnly cookies, never in localStorage.

defendQuestions.question: How would you protect the login endpoint from brute-force attacks?
defendQuestions.modelReasoning: Rate limit by IP and by email. After 5 failed attempts for the same email, introduce progressive delays (1s, 5s, 30s, lockout). After 100 failed attempts from the same IP, block the IP temporarily. Use generic error messages ('Invalid credentials') to prevent email enumeration. Consider CAPTCHA after 3 failures. Never reveal whether the email exists in login error messages.

designNotes: Never return passwords in any response
Use POST for login (creates a session/token)
Return 409 Conflict for duplicate email instead of 400
Protected endpoints need Authorization: Bearer <token> header
Password change requires both current and new password for security
